candidates.snippet:
node: ['22', '24']
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # pnpm version is read from `packageManager` in the root package.json,

candidates.matchedPattern: GitHub workflow file (path-based, no specific risk pattern)

findings.title: GitHub Actions pinned to mutable major-version tags
findings.description: The CI workflow consumes `actions/checkout@v4` (L28), `pnpm/action-setup@v4` (L32), and `actions/setup-node@v4` (L34) by mutable major-version tag. Major tags are pointers — a compromise of the maintainer account or a malicious release can move the tag to a hostile commit, and every consumer pulls it on the next run. Blast radius is limited here because the workflow only has `contents: read` (no secrets, no write tokens), so a compromised action could exfiltrate source code that's already public-or-PR-visible and tamper with build/test output, but cannot write to the repo, push branches, or steal release credentials. Still a real supply-chain weakness — and actions/checkout/setup-node ship with each new release, so once one CI pin is hostile it stays hostile until reverted.
findings.recommendation: Pin each `uses:` to a full 40-char commit SHA with the version as a trailing comment, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@692973e3d937129bcbf40652eb9f2f61becf3332 # v4.1.7`. Adopt Dependabot or Renovate to bump the SHAs (and update the comments) on a schedule.

revalidation.reasoning: The current .github/workflows/ci.yml no longer uses mutable major-version tags for the flagged actions. The workflow pins actions/checkout to 11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683, pnpm/action-setup to fc06bc1257f339d1d5d8b3a19a8cae5388b55320, and actions/setup-node to 49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020, each with a version comment. This removes the specific attack path described in the finding, where a mutable major tag such as @v4 could be repointed and automatically consumed by future CI runs. The workflow permissions remain scoped to contents: read, which would have limited the original blast radius, but the mutable-tag issue itself has been remediated in the target file. There are no imports or application data flows to trace for this YAML-only supply-chain finding; the relevant trust boundary is GitHub Actions resolving third-party action references. I attempted to check git history as requested, but the workspace does not include a .git directory, so I cannot cite the exact patch commit. Based on the current source, the vulnerable references have been replaced with full commit SHAs.
